title: What I Learned From One Month With OpenClaw
description: OpenClaw gave me an AI teammate, a security headache, and a brief god-complex, but shouldn't every influential technology?
contentMarkdown:
Getting started with your own AI agent is genuinely hard. Your agent has no idea where it is, what it's doing, or what the latest documentation says, because things are moving too fast for it to keep up. Early on, I found myself working inside Cursor just so I could search docs and see the full setup at once before ever deploying to a VPS. That friction teaches you something valuable: empathy for the context your agent is working with.

The growth that follows traces a classic S-curve. The bottom is slow and disorienting. Then you start giving your agent access to the right tools and the right context, and it starts to compound. It feels almost unsettling how capable it gets in a short window. But the curve has a ceiling. Eventually you become the bottleneck, the velocity flattens, and you're back to being the limiting factor. That steep middle section, where everything feels exponential, is probably what's driving all the hype around agents right now. It's real, just not permanent.

## The Subscription Trick I Almost Didn't Share

Something I didn't expect: my own spending anxiety was quietly killing my momentum. Every time I used the agent, there was a small hesitation in the back of my mind about cost. Once I connected OpenClaw to Codex through an OpenAI subscription, that friction disappeared. Flat-rate access meant I could experiment freely, and my progress jumped. Running a powerful agent on a subscription model feels like it shouldn't be allowed. It probably shouldn't.

## Capability vs. Security

The biggest tradeoff in any self-hosted agent setup is capability versus security. It's easy to imagine a world where you have both. In practice, they push against each other. Every time you grant the agent more access, you expand the blast radius of any mistake. Leaning into CLI and virtualization skills becomes essential here, because you're essentially onboarding a fresh worker into its own isolated environment. It sends you back to fundamentals you thought you'd moved past.

A few things worth locking down early:

- **Prompt injection** is a real attack surface, not a theoretical one
- **Sandboxing** limits how badly things can go when they do go wrong
- **Tool permissions** should default to deny for anything dangerous, and never elevate so the agent can touch the host directly
- **Browser access** is best controlled through an allowlist
- **Scope** can be set per session (higher overhead, more isolation), per agent, or shared across agents

## AI Notes Are Underrated

This is where being a little Type A pays off. If you already spend time writing down your goals, preferences, and ways of thinking, you have a rich dataset sitting there waiting. Feed that into OpenClaw and it stops feeling like a generic chatbot. It starts feeling like a teammate that actually understands what you're after. That's the difference between a tool that produces output and one that produces the *right* output. The cold, hollow feeling you get from most AI interactions comes from context poverty. Fix the context and the feeling changes entirely.

## My Setup

I run OpenClaw on a VPS with multiple agents connected to a Discord server through separate channels. It's just me and the bots. Keeping agents in separate channels prevents context overload, and each agent stays focused on its own domain. That said, I'm careful not to spin up a new agent for every little task. The setup should stay lean, or it becomes its own maintenance burden.

Everyone's configuration is going to look different, and that's the point. The real advantage of OpenClaw is that it's highly modifiable. Pick something reasonable, deploy it, and stay in the habit of looking for improvements. There's no perfect starting setup, just a starting setup.

## Homeschooling Your Agent

Think of your OpenClaw instance like a kid you're being a little overprotective of. Inside the house, it can roam freely. You might let it run a quick errand on its own every now and then. But the outside world is messy and full of bad actors, and you want it to be capable before it faces that. Build it up carefully, give it the right tools, and keep the perimeter tight until it's ready. The goal is a capable agent, not a wide-open one.

## Why You Actually Need This Level of Control

Setting up OpenClaw is a lot like building your own PC. Most people don't need to, and that's fine. But the people who are pushing the technology forward are the ones who insist on doing it themselves, not because it's easier, but because that level of customizability is the only way to actually move the frontier. Off-the-shelf agents are great for off-the-shelf problems. If you're reading this, you're probably not solving those.
